coredata.dc:title: Deciphering photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue by surface-tailored nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots derived from Kraft lignin
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Lingyun
Yu, Yuanyuan
Liang, Zhanming
Hou, Hewei
Liu, Xi
Chen, Changzhou
Min, Douyong
coredata.dc:description: Lignin in black liquor can be used to manufacture carbon nanomaterials on a large scale. However, the effect of nitrogen doping on the physicochemical properties and photocatalytic performance of carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) remains to be explored. In this study, NCQDs with different properties were prepared hydrothermally by using kraft lignin as the raw material and EDA as a nitrogen dopant. The amount of EDA added affects the carbonization reaction and surface state of NCQDs. Raman spectroscopy showed that the surface defects increased from 0.74 to 0.84. Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) showed that NCQDs had different intensities of fluorescence emission at 300–420 nm and 600–900 nm. Meanwhile, NCQDs can photo-catalytically degrade 96 % of MB under simulated sunlight irradiation within 300 min. After three months of storage, the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs remained above 94 %, showing remarkable fluorescence stability. After four times of recycling, the photo-degradation rate of NCQDs was maintained above 90 %, confirming its outstanding stability. As a result, a clear understanding of the design of carbon-based photo-catalyst fabricated from the waste of the paper-making industry has been gained.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin
Carbon quantum dots
Photocatalysis

Performance of NCQDs in MB degradation
Performance of CQDs in MB degradation
A proposed mechanism on degradation of MB catalyzed by CQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported financially by the China National Key Research and Development Program (2022YFC2105503), the Natural Science Foundation of China (31400514), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi (2018JJA130224), the Foundation of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control (ZR201805-7, 2019ZR05), and the Opening Project of National Enterprise Technology Center of Guangxi Bossco Environmental Protection Technology Co. Ltd. Nanning 530007, China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CQDs0 (a), NCQDs4 (b), NCQDs12 (c) and NCQDs20 (d).
XRD patterns of CQDs0, NCQDs4, NCQDs12 and NCQDs20.
Raman spectrum of CQDs0 (a), NCQDs4 (b), NCQDs12 (c) and NCQDs20 (d).
FT-IR spectrum of CQDs0, NCQDs4, NCQDs12 and NCQDs20.
XPS spectrum of CQDs and NCQDs (a), and high-resolution XPS spectra C1s regions of CQDs0 (b), NCQDs4 (c), NCQDs12 (d), and NCQDs20 (e).
High resolution XPS spectra N1s regions of CQDs0 (a), NCQDs4 (b), NCQDs12 (c) and NCQDs20 (d), and the content of nitrogen in NCQDs (e).
UV–vis spectra of the samples (inset: the NCQDs20 was under daylight and 365 nm UV irradition, respectively).
Photoluminescence emission spectra of CQDs0 (a), NCQDs4 (b), NCQDs12 (c) and NCQDs20 (d) at excitation wavelengths between 300 and 420 nm.
Photoluminescence emission of CQDs0 (a), NCQDs4 (b), NCQDs12 (c), and NCQDs20 (d) at excitation wavelengths between from 600 to 900 nm.

(a) The photocatalytic degradation of MB (20 ppm) by the samples under simulated sunlight irradiation; (b) UV–vis spectra of MB degraded by NCQDs20; (c) the photocatalytic degradation of MB, and (d) kinetic curves of photocatalytic degradation of MB by NCQDs20.
The proposed degradation process of MB catalyzed by CQDs.
Elemental analysis of CQD and NCQDs.

head.ce:title: Deciphering photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue by surface-tailored nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots derived from Kraft lignin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Bossco Environmental Protection Technology Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Bossco Environmental Protection Technology Co., Ltd, Nanning 530007, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Light Industry and Food Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lignin in black liquor can be used to manufacture carbon nanomaterials on a large scale. However, the effect of nitrogen doping on the physicochemical properties and photocatalytic performance of carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) remains to be explored. In this study, NCQDs with different properties were prepared hydrothermally by using kraft lignin as the raw material and EDA as a nitrogen dopant. The amount of EDA added affects the carbonization reaction and surface state of NCQDs. Raman spectroscopy showed that the surface defects increased from 0.74 to 0.84. Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) showed that NCQDs had different intensities of fluorescence emission at 300–420 nm and 600–900 nm. Meanwhile, NCQDs can photo-catalytically degrade 96 % of MB under simulated sunlight irradiation within 300 min. After three months of storage, the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs remained above 94 %, showing remarkable fluorescence stability. After four times of recycling, the photo-degradation rate of NCQDs was maintained above 90 %, confirming its outstanding stability. As a result, a clear understanding of the design of carbon-based photo-catalyst fabricated from the waste of the paper-making industry has been gained.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollution is discharged in large quantities as social industrialization progresses. These pollutants have a range of detrimental impacts on human health as well as serious harm to the ecology [1,2]. For example, methylene blue, one of the most commonly used pollutants in the textile industry, is one of the main pollutants of aquatic organisms, but excessive exposure is harmful to the human body, leading to allergies, dermatitis, endocrine interference, cardiovascular diseases, and so on [2,3]. Consequently, it is crucial to prevent and manage organic contamination effectively. Regarding the generation of sustainable and clean energy, photocatalysis is a “friendly” energy conversion technique [4]. Usually, the photocatalytic reaction is a semiconductor photocatalytic material as the catalyst, converting organic materials into carbon dioxide and water with the use of light energy. Zinc oxide, titanium dioxide, zirconia, cadmium sulfide, and other semiconductor photocatalysts have been created in recent years. The majority of semiconductor photocatalysts in use today have large band gaps and high ratios of electron-hole recombination, which restrict how effectively they can be excited to a very little quantity of visible light and ultraviolet light [5], affecting light absorption and utilization. A good photocatalyst should have good chemical stability, be reasonably inexpensive, and mix easily in liquids. Additionally, for solar energy to be used effectively in practical applications, it has to have a wide spectrum of light absorption and a high photocarrier separation efficiency [6].

CQDs is a unique zero-dimensional carbon-based material [7,8] that has unique physical and chemical properties, such as outstanding optical properties, excellent water solubility, and high optical stability [9,10]. Compared with organic probes and conventional semiconductor quantum dots, it also has low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility. It has thus been widely used in fields such as energy development, phototherapy, biosensing, medical imaging, and environmental monitoring [11,12]. Particularly, CQDs possess excellent electrical and optical qualities, and because of their outstanding light- and electron-capture and transmission abilities, they can be very helpful in enhancing photocatalytic activity [13]. Their ability to transfer electrons stems from their sp2 hybridization [14]. In addition, due to surface defects, their unique upconversion photoluminescence (UCPL) effect can the transformation of UV light into visible light and take advantage of the broad solar spectrum [15]. These capabilities can get around the limitations of photocatalysis and boost its effectiveness.

The properties of CQDs are determined by the conditions of the synthetic reaction. At present, CQDs preparation can be divided into “top-down” and “bottom-up” methods [16,17]. In contrast, CQDs synthesized by bottom-up strategies [18] are more accurate and controllable, such as CQDs synthesized by hydrothermal carbonization of small organic molecules [19]. But most organic molecules are expensive and highly toxic, so finding low-cost and environmentally friendly precursors is crucial [20,21]. Many research groups have been studying the preparation material of CQDs in recent years, such as apple juice [22], honey [23], peel [24], grass [25], etc.

The paper-making industry produces a tremendous amount of lignin as by-products every year. Lignin is a natural biomass material with good biocompatibility [26], high carbon content [27], and rich functional groups, which is an excellent source of carbon precursors of CQDs [28,29]. For example, Zhang et al. [30] prepared CQDs from alkali lignin by hydrothermal treatment for electrocatalysis. Shi et al. [31] prepared Al-CQDs for cell imaging by carbonization and hydrothermal treatment of ammoniated lignin. To the best of our knowledge, few studies have investigated the intrinsic relationship between CQDs and its performance based on the morphological characteristics after doping of nitrogen. Therefore, the effect of the addition of ethylenediamine on the surface states and carbon cores of lignin-based carbon quantum dots was systematically investigated. The effects of N doping on the structural and optical properties of CQDs were investigated by XRD, Raman, FT-IR, XPS, TEM, UV–Vis and PL, and the degradation of MB photo-catalyzed by the prepared NCQDs was evaluated. The corresponding mechanism of CQDs photocatalytic activity was elucidated.

Black liquor was kindly provided by Guitang Company (Guangxi, China). Ethylenediamine anhydrous (EDA, 99 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 38 %), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98 %), potassium bromide (KBr, 99 %), and methylene blue (MB, 96 %) were purchased from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. All chemicals were analytically pure and were used without further purification. All experiments used deionized water prepared by a Milli-Q purification system (Darmstadt Merck Company, Germany).

The Kraft lignin was precipitated according to the conventional method [32]. Briefly, the black liquid was diluted 10 times with distilled water before being acidified to pH = 2 by sulfuric acid (50 wt%). Then, the suspension was centrifugated to obtain lignin which was subquently washed by de-ionized water and diluted acid three time. After 8 h vacuum drying at 40 °C, the crude Kraft lignin (L0) was obtained. The purification of lignin was completed as follows. 5 g of L0 was dissolved in 17.5 mL of methanol-acetone solution (7: 3, v/v) which was dropping added to 175 mL ether. After that, the centrifugation was used to collect the precipitated lignin which was washed by ether 3 times. After 8 h vacuum drying at 40 °C, the purified Kraft lignin (KL) was achieved and used as the starting materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were prepared as follows. 0.5 g of KL mixed with 50 mL different concentration EDA solution (0, 4, 12, 20 %) which was treated by 10 min ultrasonication. Then, the suspension was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel reactor and heated in an autoclave for 12 h at 180 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting suspension was then filtered through a 0.22 μm filter member to get rid of aggregated particles. The filtration was dialyzed for 5 d using a dialysis bag (MWCO 3500 Da). Finally, the carbon dots were obtained after 2 d freeze-drying. The products were labeled as NCQDsA, in which A was the concentration of EDA.

The morphology of the samples was characterized by a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM, TECNAI G2 F30, FEI, America) at 300 kV. The crystallinity of samples was examined by an XRD diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8, BRUKER AXS GMBH, Germany) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm, scan rate = 5°/min). The Raman spectra of samples were recorded by a Raman spectroscopy (LabRAM, Via Reflex, Renishaw, England) and the exciting wavelength was 532 nm. The FT-IR spectra of samples were acquired by a FT-IR spectroscopy (FT-IR, TENSOR II, BRUKER AXS GMBH, Germany). The surface composition of samples were examined using an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250XI+, Thermo Fisher Scientific, America) with a monochromatic Al Kα source. The zeta potential of samples were determined by a Zeta potential analyzer (Zeta potential, Nano-ZS90X, Malvern Panalytical, England). The UV spectra of samples were recorded by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–vis, Specond plus 50, Analytik Jena, Germany). The photoluminescence spectra of samples were measured by a fluorescence spectrometer (PL, RF-5301PC, Hitachi, Japan) with a xenon lamp excitation source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of NCQDs in MB degradation
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic performance of samples were performed with the irradiation-induced by the simulated sunlight as follows. In each experiment, 50 mL of MB solution (20 ppm) was added with 30 mg sample which was stirred for 60 min to achieve the adsorption equilibrium in dark. Subsequently, photocatalytic degradation experiments were completed at ambient temperature and 2.5 mL of the solution was withdrawn at 20 min intervals and filtered across a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The absorbance of samples was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer to determine the degradation rate of MB. Each experiment was triplicated and the average, as well as the standard deviation, were presented. The degradation (
C t
/
C 0
) of MB was calculated using Eq. (1).
(1)
Degradation
C t
/
C 0
=
C t
/
C 0
=
A t
/
A 0
where
C 0
is the initial MB concentration at t = 0 and
C t
is the MB concentration during reaction at the time of t, and
A 0
and
A t
are the corresponding absorbance [33].

Fig. 1 shows the size and morphologies of samples. It was observed that the size of CQDs was measured as 2–3 nm (Fig. 1a and b), while the size of NCQDs was calculated as 4–5 nm (Fig. 1c and d). The result revealed the lignin based carbon quantum dots were successfully fabricated by one pot hydrothermal process [34], and the good dispersion of samples was chieved throughout their environment.

As illustrated in Fig. 2 , the peak at 26° was observed from CQDs, while the peak at 23° was observed from NCQDs which were attributed to (002) diffraction peaks of graphite, and presenting d spacing of 0.342 nm (CQDs) and 0.392 nm (NCQDs). The values were a little bit greater than the spacing of bulk graphite (0.335 nm) [35]. It was also demonstrated that CQDs and NCQDs are mostly highly disordered carbons with mainly amorphous structures. The results indicated that the synthesized CQDs or NCQDs were amorphous and that the carbon source in lignin had been successfully converted to CQDs or NCQDs.

Raman spectra were used to further identify the structural characteristics of the samples. As shown in Fig. 3 , the peaks at 1350 cm−1 (D-band) and 1581 cm−1 (G-band) were corresponded to the vibrations of the disordered sp3 hybrid carbon atoms and the ordered sp2 hybrid carbon atoms [36]. Furthermore, surface defects of CQDs and NCQDs samples can be calculated by the intensity of D band (ID) and G band (IG) [37]. As shown in Table S1, the ID/IG ratios were measured as 0.823, 0.762, 0.803 and 0.838 for CQDs0, NCQDs4, NCQDs12 and NCQDs20, respectively. This results showed that NCQDs20 had more surface defects than other samples. Therefore, the doping of nitrogen by EDA can effectively increase the surface defects of the samples.

The elemental analysis (EA) of the samples was shown in Table 1 . As EDA adding, the content of carbon decreased from 45.628 % to 40.584 %, while the nitrogen content increased from 0.525 % to 7.642 %, confirming the doping of nitrogen [38]. And there is little excess elements in samples, indicating the high purity of samples.

The functional groups on the samples was analyzed by FT-IR analysis, and the results were demonstrated in Fig. 4 . It was observed that the peaks at 1598 cm−1 were attributed to the aromatic CC tensile vibration, and the peaks at 2835 cm−1 and 771 cm−1 were attributed to the aromatic CH in-plane and out-of-plane deformation. The results indicated that CQDs and NCQDs were mainly composed of an aromatic backbone. Besides, the broad absorption band at 3378 cm−1 was attributed to OH tensile vibration, while the peaks at 1462 cm−1 and 1047 cm−1 were attributed to CO and CO tensile vibration, respectively. The results revealed that hydroxyl, carboxyl and carbonyl groups were presented in CQDs and NCQDs. Compared with CQDs, NCQDs contained more oxygen-containing groups, implying NCQDs had better solubility. In addition, the stretching vibrations of NH and CN at 3261 cm−1, 2935 cm−1 and 1363 cm−1 confirmed the doping of nitrogen on NCQDs. Compared with CQDs, the intensity of peaks of CN and NH increased, while the intensity of peaks of CC decreased in NCQDs, which indicated that cleavage of CC and hydrothermal alkali activation in NCQDs occurred during the hydrothermal process as EDA was applied [34].

The zeta potential analysis showed that CQDs and NCQDs were negatively charged in aqueous solution. Fig. S1 shows that the zeta potentials of CQDs0, NCQDs4, NCQDs12, and NCQDs20 were − 7.1, −7.9, −8.32, and − 10.2 mV, respectively. This result showed that the addition of EDA can increase the surface negative charge of NCQDs, improving the dispersion stability due to the intermolecular repulsion. Furthermore, the negative charge also confirmed the presence of oxygen-containing groups on surface of NCQDs.

The surface functional groups of the samples were characterized by XPS. As seen in Fig. 5a, the main peaks of C1s, O1s and N1s were observed at 285, 533, and 400 eV, respectively. The result indicated that CQDs was mainly composed of C and O. After EDA was applied, the nitrogen content was increased in NCQDs. Fig. 5b-e reveals that the CN bond was presented in NCQDs, confirming the doping of nitrogen. The results suggested that the doping of nitrogen decreased CC content while increased C-O-C and CN contents.

Fig. 6a-d shows that little peak assigned to N1s was observed from CQDs, however, the peaks at 399.15, 399.74, 401.30, and 406.56 eV attributing to pyridine N, pyrrole N, graphite N, and nitro N, respectively were identified from NCQDs. The quantification of different N configurations in NCQDs were calculated and demonstrated in Fig. 6e, and it clearly manifested the contents of pyridine N, pyrrole N, graphite N, and nitro N. It has been reported that pyridine N and graphite N are primarily responsible for oxygen adsorption which can potentially enhance the photocatalytic performance of NCQDs [38,39].

In Fig. 7 , the peak at 250–280 nm corresponding to the π-π* transition of CC was observed from the samples [40,41]. Compared with CQDs, a redshift was observed from NCQDs due to the doping of nitrogen. Meanwhile, the peak of NCQDs splits near the high-energy region, which can be interpreted as the existence of an epoxide structure in NCQDs [42]. In addition, the peak at 320–340 nm contributing to the n-π* transition of CO and CN was observed from NCQDs [ [34,36]]. It was worth noting that the wide peak of NCQDs at 320–340 nm showed a redshift and then a blueshift with the increasing addition of EDA. It has been reported that the graphite N leads to the redshift of CQDs, while the pyridine N leads to the blueshift [43]. Furthermore, the NCQDs20 suspension emitted a potent blue fluorescence under the ultraviolet irradiation (Fig. 7 (inset image)), indicating that the prepared NCQDs20 had substantial surface defects, causing more photons of radiation from the electrons [ [2]].

The photoluminescence of the samples were performed at different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 8 ). It was shown that the ideal excitation wavelength was redshifted from 320 to 380 nm, and the strongest emission peak was redshifted from 425 to 475 nm from CQDs0 to NCQDs20. It was worth noting that the photoluminescence intensity of samples significantly depended on the excitation wavelength, which was attributed to the hybridization of sp2 carbon clusters and small quantum dot particle dimension [15], or the surface chemical states generated by different functional groups (CO, C-O-C, and C-OH) [ [44]].

The sample also exhibited significant upconversion PL (UCPL) characteristics. Fig. 9 shows that the optimal emission peak was redshifted from 425 (CQDs0) to 475 nm (NCQDs20) as the excitation wavelength ranged from 600 to 900 nm. Moreover, the peak intensity of CQDs and NCQDs at 680 to 740 nm was higher than that at other regions, which was attributed to the multiphoton activity of the material [31,45]. The results suggested that NCQDs20 can absorb wide wavelength light, e.g. from UV to blue/red spectral range.

The effect of pH and ion concentration on the fluorescence stability of the sample was determined. As shown in Fig. S2 a–b, the intensity at 470 nm gradually increased as pH increased from 2 to 7, but decreased significantly when pH was raised to 12 (Fig. S2 b). The pH-dependent behavior of NCQDs20 can be explained by the amino and carboxyl groups on surface which were easily protonated as pH ≥ 7. Therefore, the surface state of NCQDs20 was optimal for emitting bright fluorescence at pH = 7. The effect of ionic concentration on the fluorescence stability of NCQDs20 was elucidated using different contentration NaCl solution. As shown in Fig. 10c-d, the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs20 at 470 nm was hardly affected as the concentration of NaCl increased to 2 M. The fluorescence stability of carbon quantum dots after long-term storage was also determined. After three months' storage, the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs20 remained up to 94 % (Fig. S3), indicating the fluorescence properties of NCQDs20 was little changed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of CQDs in MB degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic efficiency of the samples was investigated and shown in Fig. 10a. The concentration of MB decreased significantly within 60 min under dark which was attributed to the electrostatic attraction between the CQDs and the MB. Specifically, the overall potential of CQDs is negative while MB is a cationic dye, therefore CQDs can adsorb MB through the electrostatic attraction in dark.

As shown in Fig. 10a, in the absence of catalyst, the degradation rate of MB was <30 %, while in the presence of NCQDs20, the degradation rate increased to about 50 % within 120 min, revealing that the self-degradation of MB had has little contribution on the performance of materials, and NCQDs20 were a good photocatalyst. Compared with CQDs, NCQDs20 have better photocatalytic activity which can be attributed to the increase of surface defects and oxygen-containing groups and the change of N morphology [46]. NCQDs20 as the most excellent photocatalyst was further studied for its photocatalytic activity. As shown in Fig. 10b, with the prolongation of the reaction time, the intensity of the absorption peak of MB at 664 nm gradually decreased, meanwhile the peak position was blue-shifted, indicating that the demethylation of the MB structure occurred [2]. The results revealed that NCQDs20 had the ability to degrade MB completely.

Due to the low initial concentration of pollutants, the reaction follows a pseudo-first-order kinetic model (Eq. (2)) [ [1]].
(2)
− ln
C t
/
C 0
= − ln
A t
/
A 0
= Kt
where
C 0
is the initial MB concentration at t = 0 and
C t
is the MB concentration during reaction at the time of t, and
A 0
and
A t
are the corresponding absorbances, and k is the apparent rate constant.

As shown in Fig. 10d, the photocatalytic degradation of MB by NCQDs20 conformed to the pseudo-first-order kinetic model with a correlation coefficient of 0.9665 and a rate constant of 0.009 min−1. Compared with previous results, NCQDs20 had an outstanding photocatalytic activity (Table S2). The improved photocatalytic activity of NCQDs20 was attributed to the increase of surface defects and oxygen-containing groups and the change of N morphology. Specifically, the surface defects of NCQDs20 provided more reaction sites, which not only enhanced the adsorption of organic dyes, but also expanded the utilization of light, providing more energy for the redox process to generate more carriers, effectively improving MB degradation. In addition, the existence of NH and -COOH groups in NCQDs20 enabled NCQDs20 to generate intramolecular hydrogen bonds and induce the energy bands bending upwards, which promoted the stabilization and separation of photogenerated carriers. The photocatalytic performance of NCQDs20 was also related to the configuration of nitrogen. It has been reported that graphite N and pyridine N correspond to oxygen adsorption, and play a decisive role in photosensitivity [39,45]. Thereby, NCQDs20 possessed an excellent photocatalytic efficiency.

The recycling performance of NCQDs20 was evaluated and demonstrated in Fig. S4. It was seen that the catalytic degradation efficiency remained above 90 % after four recycles, indicating NCQDs20 has good stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A proposed mechanism on degradation of MB catalyzed by CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Photocatalysis is caused by the generated carriers, for example, excitation causes electrons to move from the valence band (VB) into the conduction band (CB), and corresponding holes h+ are generated on the VB to form highly active photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Separation of the produced electron-hole pairs and migrated to the surface of the material under the action of the internal electric field, hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and superoxide free radicals (O2 −) [6] were produced on the surface of CQDs. The involved reactions were listed in Eqs. (3)–(5), the photocatalytic degradation process of MB by CQDs was proposed in Fig. 11 .
(3)
CQDs + hv →
e −
+
h +
(4)
e −
CQDs
+
O 2
→
O 2
−
(5)
h +
H +
2
O /
OH −
→ · OH

In this study, the enhanced photodegradation of MB catalyzed by NCQDs20 can be explained as follows. The abundant defects on the surface of NCQDs20 can effectively enhance the adsorption of MB, and expand the light utilization, making it a good photosensitizer. The presence of nitrogen on the surface of NCQDs20 improved their local electrical alignment, facilitating the formation of a large-scale electric field [3,10], and promoting the electron migration during photodegradation. Thereby, the effective separation of photogenerated electrons and holes accelerated the redox reaction on the surface of NCQDs20. Furthermore, NCQDs20 also can act as electron donors and acceptors due to the presence of graphitic N and pyridine N which further impoved the its degradation performance [35,47].

In this study, CQDs doped with different amount of nitrogen were prepared hydrothermally by using lignin as raw material and EDA as dopant. As the concentration of EDA aqueous solution was 20 %, NCQDs performed the highest photoluminescence emission performance and the best photocatalytic ability. Under simulated sunlight irradiation, NCQDs20 exhibited the best photocatalytic degradation activity on MB which was completely degraded within 300 min. Its photocatalytic degradation rate constant was calculated as 0.009 min−1. As a result, NCQDs20 was an ideal potential photocatalyst for wastewater treatment. The findings in current investigation provided a reference for the development of NCQDs from readily available, cheap, and green lignocellulosic wastes, and contributed to the development of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots with high photocatalytic water purification efficiency.

Lingyun Hao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yuanyuan Yu: Formal analysis, Investigation. Zhanming Liang: Validation, Investigation. Hewei Hou: Methodology, Formal analysis. Xi Liu: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis. Changzhou Chen: Conceptualization, Validation, Supervision. Douyong Min: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported financially by the China National Key Research and Development Program (2022YFC2105503), the Natural Science Foundation of China (31400514), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi (2018JJA130224), the Foundation of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control (ZR201805-7, 2019ZR05), and the Opening Project of National Enterprise Technology Center of Guangxi Bossco Environmental Protection Technology Co., Ltd., Nanning 530007, China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2023.124958.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green and efficient synthesis of carbon quantum dots from cordia myxa L. and their application in photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes
